en.extract:
The Chinese grouse (Tetrastes sewerzowi), also known as Severtzov's grouse, is the smallest grouse in the world. The species was first discovered and described by Przewalski in 1876.
It is endemic to China, where it is considered endangered by the China Red Data Book.
The bird is named after a Russian explorer and naturalist, Nikolai Alekseevich Severtzov.


Description
The females have an average mass between 270–407 g (9.5–14.4 oz), and the males between 279–390 g (9.8–13.8 oz). The average body length is 327–384 mm (12.9–15.1 in) for females and 355–403 mm (14.0–15.9 in) for males, making it the smallest of the grouse species.
Chinese grouse are mostly brown, with lots of white and black patterning on their bellies. The males have a black chin patch, while females do not. Chinese grouse look similar to hazel grouse (Tetrastes bonasia), with a few differences in plumage colouration and pattern. Both males and females have a red comb above their eyes, though it is more prominent in the males.


Taxonomy
All grouse are in the family Phasianidae, which also includes pheasants and turkeys. Different sources place the Chinese grouse into either the Tetrastes genus along with the hazel grouse (Tetrastes bonasia), or the Bonasa genus, and there are still uncertainties about the phylogenetic relationships of these genera.


Habitat and distribution
The Chinese grouse is found exclusively in China, in the provinces of Gansu, Qinghai, Sichuan, Yunnan, and Tibet. As of the year 2000, the distribution of Chinese grouse extended to an area of 155,000 km2 (60,000 sq mi).
There are two main habitat requirements for Chinese grouse: coniferous trees for cover, and deciduous trees and shrubs for food. The species is found mostly in the mountains, between 2,400 and 4,300 m (7,900 and 14,100 ft) of elevation.


Status and conservation
The Chinese grouse is considered an endangered species in China. The population has been declining over the years. The main cause of this decline is habitat loss due to deforestation and habitat fragmentation. Current efforts are being made in China to stabilize the population, such as reducing logging.
The IUCN also recognizes that the population is declining. The Chinese grouse is listed as Near Threatened on the IUCN Red List.


Behavior


Vocalizations
The Chinese grouse, like other grouse species, use vocalizations when defending their territory. Their vocalizations can be described as a "whirring, bi-syllabic flutter-jump". These vocalizations are much less complex than those of the North American ruffed grouse. Male Chinese grouse also make an "offensive-cantus" (a series of nasal clucks) when courting females and in other instances of short-distance communication. They will also fan out their tail feathers, spread their wings, and fluff up their plumage to appear larger and more imposing.
Chinese grouse will also produce a variety of alarm calls.
Among the three species in the genus Bonasa, of which the Chinese grouse is sometimes considered to be a part of, the Chinese grouse has the least complex vocalizations.


Diet
Severtzov's grouse feed primarily on the buds and twigs of willow trees (Salix spp.) during the winter months. During the breeding season, males continue to feed primarily on willow, while females supplement their diets with seeds from the dragon spruce (Picea asperata), invertebrates, and forbs to increase nutrient and energy intake in preparation for laying eggs.


Reproduction
The breeding season for Chinese grouse begins between March and May. Males return to their breeding grounds earlier than females and begin defending their territories with vocalizations and displays. Males must compete for a chance to mate, since there are more males than females in the population. Once a female has chosen a mate, the pair will live together in the male's territory until the end of the breeding season. During the breeding season, females tend to gain mass and males tend to lose mass.
Chinese grouse usually nest at the base of trees. The females will lay eggs every other day until reaching a clutch size of 5-8 eggs. The eggs are light yellow with brown spots.
The females will then incubate the eggs for 27–29 days. When the precocial chicks hatch, they will follow the female for 2–3 months before dispersing from the territory.


References


External links
iNaturalist - Chinese grouse

The '''Chinese grouse''' ('''''Tetrastes sewerzowi'''''), also known as '''Severtzov's grouse''', is the smallest grouse in the world.<ref name=":2">{{Cite journal |last1=Sun |first1=Yue-Hua |last2=Fang |first2=Yun |last3=Swenson |first3=Jon E. |last4=Klaus |first4=Siegfried |last5=Zheng |first5=Guang-Mei |date=2005 |title=Morphometrics of the Chinese grouse Bonasa sewerzowi |url=https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s10336-004-0046-5 |journal=Journal of Ornithology |language=en |volume=146 |issue=1 |pages=24–26 |doi=10.1007/s10336-004-0046-5 |bibcode=2005JOrni.146...24S |issn=2193-7206|url-access=subscription }}</ref> The species was first discovered and described by [[Nikolay Przhevalsky|Przewalski]] in 1876.<ref name=":3">{{Cite journal |last=Sun |first=Yue-Hua |date=2000 |title=Distribution and status of the Chinese grouse Bonasa sewerzowi |url=https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.2981/wlb.2000.026 |journal=Wildlife Biology |language=en |volume=6 |issue=4 |pages=271–275 |doi=10.2981/wlb.2000.026 |issn=1903-220X|doi-access=free }}</ref>

It is endemic to China, where it is considered endangered by the ''China Red Data Book.''<ref name=":4">{{Cite journal |last1=Sun |first1=Yue-Hua |last2=Fang |first2=Yun |date=2010 |title=Chinese Grouse (Bonasa sewerzowi): its natural history, behavior and conservation |url=http://www.j-avianres.com/en/article/doi/10.5122/cbirds.2010.0015 |journal=Avian Research |language=en |volume=1 |issue=3 |pages=215–220 |doi=10.5122/cbirds.2010.0015 |bibcode=2010AvRes...1..215S |issn=2055-6187|url-access=subscription }}</ref>

The bird is named after a [[Russia]]n [[explorer]] and [[Natural history|naturalist]], [[Nikolai Alekseevich Severtzov]].<ref name="ODBBN">{{cite book |last=Lockwood |first=W B |title=The Oxford Dictionary of British Bird Names |publisher=[[OUP]] |year=1993 |isbn=978-0-19-866196-2}}</ref>

== Description ==
The females have an average mass between {{cvt|270|-|407|g}}, and the males between {{cvt|279|-|390|g}}.<ref name=":2" /> The average body length is {{cvt|327|-|384|mm}} for females and {{cvt|355|-|403|mm}} for males, making it the smallest of the grouse species.<ref name=":2" />

Chinese grouse are mostly brown, with lots of white and black patterning on their bellies.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Severtzov's Grouse - eBird |url=https://ebird.org/ebird/species/sevgro1 |access-date=2024-10-15 |website=ebird.org |language=en}}</ref> The males have a black chin patch, while females do not.<ref name=":5">{{Cite journal |last1=Yang |first1=Chen |last2=Wang |first2=Jie |last3=Fang |first3=Yun |last4=Sun |first4=Yue-Hua |date=2013 |title=Is Sexual Ornamentation an Honest Signal of Male Quality in the Chinese Grouse (Tetrastes sewerzowi)? |journal=PLOS ONE |language=en |volume=8 |issue=12 |article-number=e82972 |doi=10.1371/journal.pone.0082972 |issn=1932-6203 |pmc=3873284 |pmid=24386132 |doi-access=free|bibcode=2013PLoSO...882972Y }}</ref> Chinese grouse look similar to hazel grouse (''[[Hazel grouse|Tetrastes bonasia]]''), with a few differences in plumage colouration and pattern.<ref>{{Cite web |last1=de Juana |first1=Eduardo |last2=Kirwan |first2=Guy M. |date=March 4, 2020 |title=Severtov's Grouse |url=https://birdsoftheworld-org.proxy3.library.mcgill.ca/bow/species/sevgro1/cur/introduction |access-date=October 10, 2024 |website=Birds of the World}}</ref> Both males and females have a red comb above their eyes, though it is more prominent in the males.<ref name=":5" />

== Taxonomy ==
All grouse are in the family Phasianidae, which also includes pheasants and turkeys.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Gutiérrez |first1=R.J. |last2=Barrowclough |first2=George F. |last3=Groth |first3=Jeffrey G. |date=2000 |title=A classification of the grouse (Aves: Tetraoninae) based on mitochondrial DNA sequences |url=https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.2981/wlb.2000.017 |journal=Wildlife Biology |language=en |volume=6 |issue=4 |pages=205–211 |doi=10.2981/wlb.2000.017 |bibcode=2000WildB...6..205G |issn=1903-220X}}</ref> Different sources place the Chinese grouse into either the ''[[Tetrastes]]'' genus along with the [[hazel grouse]] (''Tetrastes bonasia''), or the ''Bonasa'' genus, and there are still uncertainties about the phylogenetic relationships of these genera.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Luo |first1=Xuequn |last2=Zhou |first2=Hang |last3=Li |first3=Ao |last4=Huang |first4=Zhaoxia |last5=Zhang |first5=Wenping |last6=Du |first6=Shizhang |last7=Hua |first7=Donglai |title=Description of the mitochondrial genomes of Sichuan Tetrastes sewerzowi (Galliformes: Tetraonidae) and phylogenetic relationship |journal=Scientific Reports |date=2024 |volume=14 |issue=1 |article-number=26788 |doi=10.1038/s41598-024-77604-2|doi-access=free |pmid=39500940 |pmc=11538510 |bibcode=2024NatSR..1426788L }}</ref>

== Habitat and distribution ==
The Chinese grouse is found exclusively in China, in the provinces of Gansu, Qinghai, Sichuan, Yunnan, and Tibet.<ref name=":3" /> As of the year 2000, the distribution of Chinese grouse extended to an area of {{cvt|155000|km2}}.<ref name=":3" />

There are two main habitat requirements for Chinese grouse: coniferous trees for cover, and deciduous trees and shrubs for food.<ref name=":3" /> The species is found mostly in the mountains, between {{cvt|2400|and|4300|m}} of elevation.<ref name=":3" />

=== Status and conservation ===
The Chinese grouse is considered an endangered species in China.<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal |last1=Sun |first1=Yue-Hua |last2=Swenson |first2=Jon E. |last3=Fang |first3=Yun |last4=Klaus |first4=Siegfried |last5=Scherzinger |first5=Wolfgang |date=April 2003 |title=Population ecology of the Chinese grouse, Bonasa sewerzowi, in a fragmented landscape |journal=Biological Conservation |language=en |volume=110 |issue=2 |pages=177–184 |doi=10.1016/S0006-3207(02)00187-8|bibcode=2003BCons.110..177S }}</ref> The population has been declining over the years. The main cause of this decline is habitat loss due to [[deforestation]] and habitat [[Habitat fragmentation|fragmentation]]. Current efforts are being made in China to stabilize the population, such as reducing logging.<ref name=":1">{{Cite journal |last=Storch |first=Ilse |date=July 2007 |title=Conservation Status of Grouse Worldwide: An Update |journal=Wildlife Biology |language=en |volume=13 |issue=sp1 |pages=5–12 |doi=10.2981/0909-6396(2007)13[5:CSOGWA]2.0.CO;2 |issn=0909-6396 |s2cid=19449634 |doi-access=free}}</ref>

The [[International Union for Conservation of Nature|IUCN]] also recognizes that the population is declining.<ref name=iucn/> The Chinese grouse is listed as [[Near-threatened species|Near Threatened]] on the [[IUCN Red List]].

== Behavior ==

=== Vocalizations ===
The Chinese grouse, like other grouse species, use vocalizations when defending their territory. Their vocalizations can be described as a "whirring, bi-syllabic flutter-jump".<ref name=":6">{{Cite journal |last1=Scherzinger |first1=Wolfgang |last2=Klaus |first2=Siegfried |last3=Sun |first3=Yue-Hua |last4=Fang |first4=Yun |date=2006 |title=Ethological and acoustical characters of the Chinese grouse (Bonasa sewerzowi), compared with sibling hazel grouse (B. bonasia) and ruffed grouse (B. umbellus) |url=https://avibirds.com/wp-content/uploads/pdf/hazelhoen1.pdf |journal=Acta Zoologica Sinica |pages=293–297 |via=avibirds.com}}</ref> These vocalizations are much less complex than those of the North American [[ruffed grouse]]. Male Chinese grouse also make an "offensive-cantus" (a series of nasal clucks) when [[Courtship display|courting]] females and in other instances of short-distance communication.<ref name=":6" /> They will also fan out their tail feathers, spread their wings, and fluff up their plumage to appear larger and more imposing.

Chinese grouse will also produce a variety of alarm calls.

Among the three species in the genus ''Bonasa'', of which the Chinese grouse is sometimes considered to be a part of, the Chinese grouse has the least complex vocalizations.<ref name=":6" />

=== Diet ===
Severtzov's grouse feed primarily on the buds and twigs of willow trees (''[[Willow|Salix spp.]]'') during the winter months.<ref name=":4" /> During the breeding season, males continue to feed primarily on willow, while females supplement their diets with seeds from the dragon spruce (''[[Picea asperata]]''), invertebrates, and [[forb]]s to increase nutrient and energy intake in preparation for laying eggs.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Jie |first1=Wang |last2=Chen |first2=Yang |last3=Nan |first3=Lu |last4=Yun |first4=Fang |last5=Yue-Hua |first5=Sun |date=2010 |title=Diet of Chinese Grouse (Tetrastes sewerzowi) during Preincubation |url=http://www.bioone.org/doi/abs/10.1676/09-060.1 |journal=The Wilson Journal of Ornithology |language=en |volume=122 |issue=1 |pages=177–180 |doi=10.1676/09-060.1 |issn=1559-4491|url-access=subscription }}</ref>

=== Reproduction ===
The breeding season for Chinese grouse begins between March and May.<ref name=":4" /> Males return to their breeding grounds earlier than females and begin defending their territories with vocalizations and displays. Males must compete for a chance to mate, since there are more males than females in the population.<ref name=":4" /> Once a female has chosen a mate, the pair will live together in the male's territory until the end of the breeding season. During the breeding season, females tend to gain mass and males tend to lose mass.<ref name=":2" />

Chinese grouse usually nest at the base of trees.<ref name=":4" /> The females will lay eggs every other day until reaching a clutch size of 5-8 eggs. The eggs are light yellow with brown spots.<ref name=":2" />

The females will then incubate the eggs for 27–29 days.<ref name=":4" /> When the precocial chicks hatch, they will follow the female for 2–3 months before dispersing from the territory.

zh.extract:
斑尾榛鸡（学名：Tetrastes sewerzowi）为松鸡科榛鸡属的鸟类，俗名羊角鸡，是中国的特有物种。分布于甘肃、青海、四川等地，主要生活于山柳灌丛、金腊梅灌丛、杜鹃灌丛、云杉林以及赤杨林。该物种的模式产地在甘肃山脉。


保护
中国国家重点保护动物等级：一级


亚种
斑尾榛鸡指名亚种（学名：Tetrastes sewerzowi sewerzowi），是中国的特有物种。分布于甘肃、青海、四川等地。该物种的模式产地在甘肃山脉。
斑尾榛鸡四川亚种（学名：Tetrastes sewerzowi secunda），是中国的特有物种。分布于四川等地。该物种的模式产地在西康附近。


参考文献
